intro.title: Articles: a, an, the and no article

sections.title: What articles do (core logic)
sections.content:
Before almost every noun: a three-way choice — **a / an**, **the**, or **∅** (no article). The signal: *is this identifiable to you?*
- Articles sit in the Noun Phrase root (#3 in the Tree Model) — the **most frequent grammatical decision in English**: several times per sentence, almost every time you open your mouth.
- B2 exceptions and edge cases (abstract nouns generic vs specific, *the rich / the poor*, rivers and mountains, newspaper and hotel names): [[topic:articles_advanced|Articles — extension]]. Master the core first.

steps.q: Can the listener identify exactly which one? (already mentioned, unique, or specified by context)
Singular and countable? (first mention, one of many)

steps.end: ∅ — no article (uncountables and plurals in general, names, most countries)

diagrams.title: The three-way choice at a glance
rows: Singular countable, first mention, one of many
Listener can identify which one (mentioned, unique, specified)
**The** sun. **The** car I bought is red.
Plural / uncountable in general, most names
Cats are animals. Go to work. Prague.

diagrams.text: Czech has **no articles at all** — the single hardest English structure for Czech speakers to acquire. Good news: ~80% of cases are covered by the core rules in this topic.

sections.title: a / an — first mention, indefinite, "one of many"
sections.content:
Use **a / an** with a **singular countable noun** the listener can't yet identify — there are many possibilities, and you're picking one.
**a or an? Listen to the SOUND, not the letter.** This is rule one of every spoken exchange — get it right and you sound natural from sentence one; get it wrong and every listener notices.

diagrams.title: When to use a / an
rows: First mention in a story or conversation

diagrams.title: Sound, not spelling
rows: letter names *em-*, *ef-*, *ex-* start with vowel sounds

sections.title: the — definite, identifiable, "we both know which one"
sections.content:
Use **the** when both speaker and listener can identify **which one** — three main triggers.
Pronunciation: **"thee"** before a vowel sound (*thee apple, thee hour*), **"thuh"** before a consonant sound (*thuh book, thuh university*).

diagrams.title: Three triggers for the
rows: **(a)** Already mentioned — the second-mention rule (see the storytelling card)
I saw a dog. **The** dog was barking.
**(b)** Only one in context — unique by nature or by situation
**The** sun. **The** moon. **The** manager. Close **the** door. (this room's door)
**(c)** Defined by what follows — a phrase or clause that pins it down
**The** book I bought yesterday. **The** capital of France. **The** water in this bottle.

groups.label: some place names with common-noun elements

diagrams.text: Czech speakers tend to **under-use the**. Every reference back to something already known needs the signal — missing it doesn't usually break meaning, but it sounds noticeably foreign in every email, exam answer and conversation.

sections.title: No article (∅) — general, abstract, plural, uncountable
sections.content: Use **no article** (∅) when you're talking about something in **general**, not a specific instance — five categories.

diagrams.title: ∅ in general
rows: ∅ Water is essential. ∅ Information is power.
∅ Dogs are friendly. ∅ Children need sleep.
**(d)** Names — most personal names, cities, countries, months

diagrams.text: This is the rule Czech speakers find most natural — Czech also says these without an article.

sections.title: ∅ general vs the specific — the key contrast
sections.content:
**But beware:** the moment the noun becomes specific — defined by context or a following phrase — English switches to **the**.
General → ∅, specific → *the*: the most important pair in this topic.

right.title: the — specific
points: **The water** in this bottle is cold. (this specific water)
**The dogs** next door bark all night. (these specific dogs)

sections.title: a/an → the (the storytelling rule)
sections.content:
**The most fundamental discourse pattern in English**: **a/an** at first mention, then **the** every time after — now we both know which one.
- *I saw **a** dog in the park. **The** dog was chasing **a** squirrel. **The** squirrel ran up **a** tree. **The** tree was huge.*
This single pattern is behind a huge proportion of *a* vs *the* decisions — every story, news article, email and conversation is knitted together this way. If you only remember **one rule** about articles, remember this one.

items.label: first mention
items.formula: a / an (or ∅ for plurals & uncountables) → new to the listener

items.label: now identifiable → the
items.formula: Did you see **the** phone I bought?

items.label: part of the known phone → the
plurals/uncountables: ∅ first, then the
items.formula: I bought ∅ flowers for my mother. → **The** flowers were beautiful.

sections.title: Articles with names — the ∅ default
sections.content: Most **names** take **no article** — ∅ is the default.

groups.label: single mountains and lakes (with their generic word)

sections.title: Names that take the — learnable categories
sections.content:
A small, learnable set of names takes **the**.
*(Newspapers, hotels, ships, institutions, and the full geography list are in [[topic:articles_advanced|Articles — extension]].)*

groups.label: countries with a common noun in the name
mountain ranges and groups of islands

items.good: I live in the Czech Republic.
items.why: common noun in the name → the

diagrams.text: *"I live in Czech Republic"* and *"I went to the London"* are two of the most instantly noticeable Czech-speaker errors — fixing this gives the most visible quick win.

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors (1–3)
sections.content: Because Czech has no articles, five mistakes appear constantly. Fix these and your English improves visibly within a week.

diagrams.title: 3 · a/an based on letter, not sound

sections.title: Czech-speaker errors (4–5) and roadmap
sections.content:
Two more constant errors — then the roadmap.
**Next steps**: when the core feels automatic, move to [[topic:articles_advanced|Articles — extension]] (B2: abstract nouns generic vs specific, *the rich/the poor*, full geography list, newspaper/hotel/ship names, subjects of study, title vs role).

items.good: I live in the Czech Republic.

items.why: introducing it for the first time → a

diagrams.text: These are the exact mistakes that mark out a Czech speaker in every email, exam answer and conversation. They're not difficult — they're just unfamiliar. Practise the cards in this topic, then move on.

diagrams.title: Direct practice in this curriculum
items: Error correction: fix the wrong article

questions.question: 'I need _____ umbrella.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: Umbrella starts with a vowel sound → an.

questions.question: '_____ life is short.' (general) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Life in general (uncountable) → no article.

questions.question: 'I bought a car. _____ car is red.' Which fits in the second sentence?
questions.explanation: Second mention – we know which car → the.

questions.question: 'She goes to _____ work by bus.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: Go to work (as activity) → no article.

questions.question: 'We had _____ breakfast at eight.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: Meals in general (have breakfast) → no article.

questions.question: 'She dreams of visiting _____ Africa.' Which fits?
questions.explanation: Continents take no article → Africa, Europe, Asia.

gapfill.title: Practice: Choose a, an, the or no article (∅)

questions.question: I need _____ umbrella.
questions.explanation: Vowel sound (umbrella) → an.

questions.question: She's _____ engineer.
questions.explanation: Vowel sound (engineer) → an. One of many.

questions.question: _____ sun is very bright today.
questions.explanation: Unique – there is only one sun → the.

questions.question: We had _____ breakfast at eight.
questions.explanation: Meals in general → no article.

questions.question: He goes to _____ work by bus.
questions.explanation: Go to work (activity) → no article.

questions.question: I bought _____ new car. _____ car is red.
questions.explanation: First mention → a. Second mention (we know which one) → the.

questions.question: _____ cats are animals.
questions.explanation: Plural in general → no article.

questions.question: She has _____ hour for lunch.
questions.explanation: Hour starts with vowel sound (silent h) → an.

questions.question: Close _____ door, please.
questions.explanation: The door in this room – listener knows which one → the.

questions.question: They live in _____ USA.
questions.explanation: Country name that includes a common noun (United States) → the.

questions.question: _____ water in the bottle is cold.
questions.explanation: Specific water (in that bottle) → the.

questions.question: It's _____ best film I've ever seen.
questions.explanation: Superlative (best) → the.

questions.question: _____ life is full of surprises.
questions.explanation: Life in general (uncountable) → no article.

questions.question: I'll meet you at _____ home.
questions.explanation: At home (fixed phrase) → no article.

questions.question: She dreams of visiting _____ Africa.
questions.explanation: Continents take no article → Africa, Europe, Asia.

questions.question: Wrong: 'The cats are animals.' (meaning all cats) Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Plural in general → no article.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I went to the London.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Most city names → no article.

questions.question: Wrong: 'She goes to the work by bus.' Correct the sentence.
answers: She goes to work by bus.
She goes to work by bus
questions.explanation: Go to work (activity) → no article.

questions.question: Wrong: 'We had a breakfast at eight.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Meals in general (have breakfast) → no article.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I need a umbrella.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Vowel sound (umbrella) → an.

questions.question: Wrong: 'He is an university student.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: University starts with consonant sound (yoo-) → a.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Sun is very bright today.' Correct the sentence.
answers: The sun is very bright today.
The sun is very bright today
questions.explanation: Unique (the sun) → the.

questions.question: Wrong: 'They live in USA.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: USA (country name with common noun) → the.

questions.question: Wrong: 'He visited the Africa.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Continents take no article → Africa, Europe, Asia.

mc.title: Practice: Choose a, an, the or no article (∅) (multiple choice)

questions.question: I need _____ umbrella.
questions.explanation: Vowel sound (umbrella) → an.

questions.question: She's _____ engineer.
questions.explanation: Vowel sound (engineer) → an. One of many.

questions.question: _____ sun is very bright today.
questions.explanation: Unique – there is only one sun → the.

questions.question: We had _____ breakfast at eight.
questions.explanation: Meals in general → no article.

questions.question: He goes to _____ work by bus.
questions.explanation: Go to work (activity) → no article.

questions.question: I bought _____ new car. _____ car is red.
questions.explanation: First mention → a. Second mention (we know which one) → the.

questions.question: _____ cats are animals.
questions.explanation: Plural in general → no article.

questions.question: She has _____ hour for lunch.
questions.explanation: Hour starts with vowel sound (silent h) → an.

questions.question: Close _____ door, please.
questions.explanation: The door in this room – listener knows which one → the.

questions.question: They live in _____ USA.
questions.explanation: Country name that includes a common noun (United States) → the.
